ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Angus Beef Don or beef donburi. FOODON_00002572 (beef dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for beef donburi. A0CKP (Beef, cooked) is used as the closest parent. The dish also contains rice and egg components not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for beef donburi or Angus Beef Don as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 550 g, comprising roughly 240 g Angus beef, 200 g steamed Japanese rice, one onsen egg (~60 g), shiitake mushrooms, crispy garlic, spring onions, and beni shoga, as described in the field dataing.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef, short loin, top loin steak, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/8 inch fat, all grades, cooked, grilled (FDC ID 174033); Rice, white, short-grain, cooked (FDC ID 169760); Egg, whole, cooked, poached (FDC ID 173424); composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Angus Beef Don as a composite dish. Per-100g values are estimated by weighted-ingredient composition: approximately 44% cooked Angus beef (USDA FDC 174033), 36% cooked short-grain white rice (USDA FDC 169760), 11% onsen egg (USDA FDC 173424), 5% shiitake mushrooms cooked (USDA FDC 169243), and 4% condiments/garnish (shoyu, beni shoga, spring onions, crispy garlic). Sodium is elevated due to shoyu-based seasoning. Values are rounded to one decimal place.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — White rice, Japanese short-grain, cooked (GI ~54); composite dish GL estimated from carbohydrate content per serving
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Angus Beef Don as a composite dish. GI of 54 is assigned based on the dominant glycemic carbohydrate contributor being Japanese short-grain white rice (GI ~54 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021). The beef, egg, mushroom, and condiment components contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (54 × 84.7) / 100 ≈ 45.7, rounded to 45.6. Available carbohydrate per serving estimated as 15.4 g/100g × 550 g serving = 84.7 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from shoyu-based seasoning and beni shoga
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to an estimated sodium content of approximately 310 mg per 100 g (approximately 1705 mg per serving), which represents a substantial proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced-soy-sauce preparation may lower sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate load from Japanese short-grain rice with moderate glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a standard serving provides approximately 85 g of available carbohydrate with a glycemic load of approximately 46, which is high; portion reduction of the rice component and pairing with the protein-rich beef and egg can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from Angus beef and elevated sodium from shoyu seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should note that this dish provides approximately 2.3 g saturated fat per 100 g and an estimated 1705 mg sodium per serving; choosing leaner beef cuts and requesting reduced-sodium seasoning would improve the cardiovascular risk profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Angus Beef Don (アンガスビーフ丼) is a Japanese-style beef rice bowl (donburi) featuring approximately 240 g of sliced or braised Angus beef served over a bed of steamed Japanese short-grain rice, accompanied by shoyu-marinated shiitake mushrooms, a soft onsen egg (slow-cooked at low temperature to achieve a custard-like yolk), crispy fried garlic, sliced spring onions, and beni shoga (Japanese pickled red ginger). The dish is a premium restaurant interpretation of the classic gyudon format, distinguished by the use of Angus beef for its marbling and flavour depth. The beef is typically simmered or pan-seared and glazed with a shoyu-mirin-based tare sauce, imparting a savoury-sweet umami profile. Texturally, the dish combines tender beef, soft rice, silky egg yolk, crunchy garlic, and the sharp acidic contrast of beni shoga. Nutritionally, a standard serving (~550 g) provides approximately 924 kcal, 70 g protein, 34 g fat, and 85 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, energy-dense meal. The sodium content is elevated (~1705 mg per serving) due to shoyu seasoning, which is a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic load is high (~46 per serving) primarily from the rice component, requiring portion awareness for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. This dish is served at The Oyster Bank restaurant in Singapore and is representative of Japanese donburi cuisine adapted for the Singapore dining market.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Angus Beef Don as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-ingredient composition using USDA FoodData Central entries for the individual components (cooked Angus beef, cooked short-grain white rice, poached egg, cooked shiitake mushrooms). GI is estimated from the dominant glycemic contributor (Japanese short-grain rice) using the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved sources and the ingredient proportions are informed by the restaurant description specifying 240 g beef in a ~550 g total serving.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component entries; no single approved-source record for Angus Beef Don exists. 2. Sodium is elevated due to shoyu-based seasoning; the estimate of 310 mg/100g may underestimate actual restaurant sodium if additional seasoning is applied. 3. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate based on Japanese short-grain white rice as the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source (University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021); no direct GI measurement for this composite dish exists. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and are not individually validated against a single composite-dish source. 5. Halal status is not confirmed; shoyu may contain wheat and the beef sourcing is not certified halal. 6. Price of SGD 40.48 is preserved from the field survey data source record. 7. This dish is served at The Oyster Bank - Funan, Singapore. 8. FoodOn FOODON_00002572 and FoodEx2 A0CKP are used as closest-parent ontology anchors; no exact ontology code exists for beef donburi as a composite dish.
